speaker: interviewer
text: 30 more seconds. [PAUSES FOR 31 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You are doing the five second or five-- OK, so-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Alright, ?] my name is Margaret Kocevar, and I'm here with Max Guttmann. The date is March 14th, 1996. It's Richmond Heights, Ohio, the United States of America, and the interview will be conducted in English. Can you please tell us your name and spell your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name is Max [? Marcus ?] Reuben Guttmann, G-U-T-T M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us where you were born and how you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the city of Kattowitz at that time Germany. And that's spelled, at the time, K-A-T-T-O-W-I-T-Z. It is now Poland, and it is spelled K-A-T-O-W-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birthday, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is June 7, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will be 75 years old in a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about the town in which you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town which I was born is, first of all, the youngest, I would say, Upper Silesean towns, the oldest being about 16 kilometers away, or 10 miles away-- Beuthen, which goes back to the 13th century. Kattowitz became a city only in 1865, 1863 or 1865. But it is an industrial center, surrounded by coal mines, steel mills, zinc mills, ore mines, and so on. Highly industrialized, and as of today, extremely polluted and-- and in bad shape. It began to grow, really, only when the railroad was brought through there. And by then, many of the managements from those coal mines and steel mills settled in that city. And it also became a railroad center. My family was connected with the city from the-- about 1880s or 1885. And my grandfather, as a young man-- my paternal grandfather, as a young man, came to the city as a apprentice into a hardware store. He later on built his first-- got his own store and later on built, with the help of some others, the biggest steel bar business in Upper Silesia, a distribution center where he bought  directly from the steel mills. He also was a member of the city council in that city from 1903 till 1920. And my father was born there. And my father later on worked there on and off. And the happiest time probably for him, between 1927 and 1933, when he was a-- a business editor of and deputy editor in chief of a local German language newspaper in Poland. He was a very versatile man, who also wrote theater and music critiques, and-- and was active in the neighborhood, as well in Beuthen, as in Kattowitz and in other organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I lived there on and off. When the city became Polish in 1922, one year after I was born, my grandfather wasn't there any more. He did not speak Polish, and he did not want to stay there. He had moved to Berlin. And subsequently, the company was sold to Polish partners and my parents moved to Berlin in 1923, where my father got a job, which didn't last very long because the company went broke during the inflation.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we moved back to Silesia, and this time to the city of Beuthen. That was a city where my mother was born, and my mother's family had been residing for many years. Beuthen is spelled B-E-U-T-H-E-N. And it had a suffix-- O-S, which meant Oberschlesien in German. Upper Silesia to-- in contrast to another Beuthen, which was on the Oder River. The city is now known as Bytom, B-Y-T-O-M. That is really the original name at which is was-- at which it was incorporated in the 13th century. That whole area of Upper Silesia changed hands between Germans, Poles, Austrians, Bohemians. At one time, even Duke of Luxembourg owned some of those properties there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before you go on, what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Fritz. He had a Doctorate in Political Sciences, Political Economics.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Gertrude. She was-- her maiden name was Wachsner, W-A-C-H-S-N-E-R. She was born in 1900 in Beuthen. And amazingly so, my-- her father was a, for that time, very progressive man. He insisted that his daughter have an education, which was rare at that time. Most of those, especially among Jewish people, also, thought that the-- the woman belongs in the house and in the kitchen. Education wasn't important. My father-- my grandfather said, if ever anything happens to a husband, if she gets one, but she was too young. He said if she ever gets one, she will have to be able to support herself if something happens to her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have to-- ever have to support herself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, very much so. She helped, first of all, to support the family when my father lost his job with the German-- with the German language newspaper. And after the war, when my father passed away during the war-- I think we'll get to that a little bit later-- but after the war, when she came out of-- first, when the war began, in the ghetto, she had to support herself. And then then when she came out of the camp, she had to support herself. And when she came to the United States, she had to support herself, even so after she got married a second time. Her husband in the beginning, her second husband, in the beginning, didn't earn a lot of money. And besides, she still wanted to be independent because he had his own child from his first marriage. My mother had two children from her first marriage. So she wanted to see if she can provide later on for the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to the beginning of the war, you said your mother helped out. What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at one point, she worked for a brief time in a book and paper-- a stationary store to get a little bit more acquainted with the book trade. But eventually, she opened a, which sounds funny in this country, a private lending library. And the ground stock for that came from my father's big library, which at one time was about 5,000 volumes. Then she bought more. And towards the end-- in the beginning, when the war started in 1939, the library contained about 12- 13,000 books in three languages. German, most of them. About-- probably about 40, 50% German, 30 or 45% Polish, and about 5% French. People were borrowing books and were paying either a fixed fee for a month to read as many books as they wanted. And that was, in the beginning, if I remember well, 2 zlotys a month. At that time, there were 6 zlotys to the dollar, about. And for the single book, you paid, depending on what it was, because bestsellers went for a little bit more, and the other went for a little bit less. And so that was how Mother helped before the war to support the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you help out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had the fun job to try to collect the books which people were reluctant to return. And on my bicycle, I traveled all over the surrounding suburbs and was not always friendly received by the people. [LAUGHS] There's never a good time to call on somebody and ask them, would you please return the book?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you were doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was about 16, 17 years old. I was working already. I started probably to do that when I was 15, but then later on, I was working for her, I guess, I had to work when I was 16. And then it's as on the side, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is 10-- 10 years younger. Her name is Vera, V-E-R-A. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She went to a camp at the age of 12, survived. Came early to America as a-- with the help of HIAS or Joint, whatever. Got a education in America, has a-- two bachelor degrees, a master degree in-- in librarian sciences, and worked for over 20 years for the United States Army as a civilian employee, as a librarian, the last, oh, since 1970, long-- the last 20 years in Germany, and before that, five years in Korea, and about two years in New York at the Veteran's Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and your sister get along when you were kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got along pretty well, as with a 10-year difference, age difference. At first, it was a little toy-like to-- to have around. Later on, I loved her. She loved me. She-- she-- she, especially when she got a little older, and when I had a bicycle, she would wait when I come back form work and that I put her on my bike and drive around a little bit with her. The only time she was doing mischief to me was when I was collecting postage stamps. And I had the stamps sorted out on the table, and her nose would just about reach the level of the table. And she would blow-- [LAUGHS] the stamps away, [LAUGHS] which did not make me very happy. But otherwise, we were getting along very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's not too bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't see each other very much. We parted when the-- when the war started, when she was eight. I saw her briefly when I came out of the army, and I found her. She was 14. And then she went to Germany, and then, subsequently, to America where I see her-- I didn't see her for another 13 years. And then she went to-- well, she was here in America, New York, and then she went to Korea and to  Germany. So we never saw each other a lot since we grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did-- you did have extended family prior to the war? You said you saw your grandparents. You knew your great-grandparent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had-- we had a-- big family, especially when we were living in Beuthen where my mother's family was. My father's family got kind of spread out a little bit over the world, mainly due to the fact that the border was drawn and Kattowitz became Poland. But my mother had her mother and numerous uncles. And my great-grandfather was there. And we would have Friday evening Kiddush and dinner in my grandmother's house. My grandfather had passed away in 1923, when I was little-- my maternal grandfather. And then we would go to my great-grandfather's house. And there were children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren. The house was full, and there were some very strict rules. You never sat down on the place at the table where great-grandfather was sitting. That was a place reserved for him. But he loved us kids. He would take us to his little office, because he had the business adjacent to his apartment, and call us in and open his desk drawer and take out some chocolates for us. From very little ones to bigger ones, everybody came in there. And of course, he would not smoke on Shabbos, but he had always a supply of big, black cigars. And then the men would come in, and he would pass out cigars to the men, which you were not allowed to smoke on Shabbos, of course. [LAUGHS] But they could take home with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a big family there. There was a big family. There were cousins, second cousins, and so on, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from her mother's side from the family, not too many survived. There are a few distant relatives. From my father's side more, because they, some of them, managed to get out, Australia, New Zealand, wherever, Israel. Can you tell me where you went to school at?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started school in that city of Beuthen, which I mentioned once before, where the system was what is called in the United States, separate but equal, which meant since Upper Silesia is predominantly Roman Catholic, all the Catholic kids went to the elementary schools closest to their homes. For two other confessions were separate municipal elementary schools. There was a Protestant municipal elementary school, and there was a Jewish municipal elementary school. The teachers were being paid by the city. The teachers were not all Jews. In my time, there was one Gentile teacher there. The rest of them were Jews. There was no-- were no classes on Saturday, in contrast to the other schools where, in Germany at that time, it was a six-day school week. From the second grade on, there were Hebrew classes in the afternoon, twice a week. And from the second grade on, some kids were selected to sing in the choir. There were two synagogues, the big one and a small one. And the big one was a kind of strange set up, with on the one side, women and men sitting separately, and the women on a second elevation. But there were-- the choir consisted of boys and men, boys being the sopranos and altos. No girls were allowed in my time. Later on, I found out after I left, there were some girls. But there was an organ there, and there was a Gentile organ player. And so at for Americans, this is a rather strange combination, where on-- on the one side, relatively conservative, but on the other side, a little bit kind of close to reform. [LAUGHS] But on the high holidays, my father, since he was busy working, attended temple mostly only on the holidays. My maternal grandmother was very Shabbos. She was very religious. As a matter of fact, I was afraid to go to her kitchen to not mess up the dishes between the meat and the dairy because that was a no-no. But when my father went to the temple, as it was done in Germany on the high holidays, he came in a top hat, dark coat, dark suit, and was very formal. Everybody came, elderly man came in top hats and then changed in the temple to a yarmulke, whatever. But that-- that was the custom over there. And I sang in the-- in the choir for two years. I would not say that I was the first soprano there. I think boys-- I never learned-- I liked music. I never learned, really, to read music. So I was singing more by-- by ear. But I enjoyed it. I enjoyed it tremendously. I especially enjoyed the organ, and I fell in love with organ music since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a kid were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at times, mischievous. My aunt-- and my mother's sister, at one point, when she came to visit us here in 1970 or '71, said I was a good kid. I was, at times, mischievous, I know. I-- I remember some of the things only from being told what I did. But while we were living in Berlin, I did hide, at one time, the key to the kindergarten when we moved. There was supposed to be some repair done at the building where we were. And they moved us into a quonset hut, and I didn't like that quonset hut. And I buried, allegedly, that key to the kindergarten in the garden somewhere. And when I was asked where it is, I said, I sent it to my brother. I didn't have a brother, [LAUGHTER] but they didn't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd say that was mischievous.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Beuthen, we lived on a cul-de-sac street in a residential area where there was little traffic. And so as German boys did, we played soccer on the street. And it happened that we busted a few basement windows once in a while. It was nothing malicious. We were no vandals. But when you kick a ball, various things happened. I had, always, beat up knees because I was running around. We had-- I wrote in my memoirs, when we grew up, we were not afraid. And our parents were not afraid to let us run around. We went to the park. I went to the park when I was eight years old, alone with my friends. And nobody worried that somebody is going to do any harm to us. My parents sent us to the store sometimes to do some buying if something was needed in the house and gave us a little piece of paper and some money and went to the store, and so you handed it in the store to the sales clerk and-- and buy the stuff. I mean, it was a life-- I had to walk, oh, 20, 25 minutes through most of the city to go to the elementary school. And after my mother had taken me there once, I always walked alone. I mean, I walked with my friends, but we never were worried. And our parents were not worried. The city had, at its peak, about 100,000 population. And it was a quiet city. And so life at that time, at the Weimarian-- during the Weimarian republic before Hitler came was wonderful. It became in that city very scary when a trial, which is famous in German history before Hitler came to power, took place. I think it was in 1930 or 1931 when some Nazis broke into a house and shot a man in front of his mother because they suspected him of being a communist. The people got caught. There was-- and it didn't take as long as in America. They don't wait five years for a trial. They took the guys to trial pretty fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Nazis were taken to trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was still before the Nazis came to power.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so who--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis-- the-- the court took the Nazis who shot--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, the Nazis were taken to trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were taken to trial. The name of the-- of the prosecutor was Lachman. I knew him because we had lived at one time in the same house. The process was called, known in history as the Murder of Potempa. And when it came close to announcing the sentence, there was terrible friction in the city. They had, which was rare in Germany, issued carbines to the police. They militarized the police. There were policemen all over the place. It was forbidden to congregate in the city. For more when three people got together on a street corner, they were chased apart by the police. And I remember that day very well. Now, I have to explain why we lived on Main Street in a big house, which was owned by my grandmother. In the back of that house were-- was a printing plant from a local newspaper. And in the entrance to the house, which was like a covered driveway, there were glass vitrines, where the paper always was hang out, the newspaper. So people were standing there all the time. I had, at that time, art classes, private art classes with a teacher, who would take a couple kids and do something. My mother always wanted us kids to do something, be involved in something. So I had art classes. There was an art class where my mother suddenly came and said, let's go. Go home. And we did not go the usual way, but she took me into a house on a street, which runs parallel to the street on which we were living. And we went through the house, into the backyard. There was a door in a fence. She went to the door. And suddenly, we were in the backyard of my grandmother's house. Now, that was a big house, my grandmother had. And it had two yards. There was one front yard, surrounded by buildings. And then there was an other backyard, where on the one side, my grandparents had a little piece of garden. And on the other side, there was a-- some little factory. I never knew that there existed a gate there. That-- my mother knew that because she had lived, grown up in that house. So they wouldn't let-- take me to the house from the front. We had a-- there was a big iron gate over there, which had been closed. It turned out by then, the papers, the-- the glass in the vitrines had been smashed up already because they had printed a special edition of the paper and shown that the men had been convicted. They had been found guilty and been convicted. And immediately, a riot broke out from the Nazis. So we went up in the apartment, in the back. And in the streets, it was wild. My father came in by train. We lived about five minutes from the railroad stations. My father had come in by train from work. And he got-- had a key. And he opened the door and got in the house. And some friends of the family came. And in the evening, as soon as it got dark, the Nazis were milling in the street, and were walking fore and back, and they had smashed up a couple windows in Jewish stores. And the friends of -- now, something strange happened. The friends of my parents were looking out. And one of the ladies had-- I don't know if you know what a lorgnon is. Hold it in one hand. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like eyeglasses, but you hold it on a stick. And you-- it is-- you can put together, and when you flip a switch, it opens up and you have two glasses. And that fell out. We lived what is considered in America, the third floor. It fell down in the street. My father went down, opened the door, and there was a policeman standing, and said, you were lucky. It didn't hit anybody, and it didn't break. And gave it to my father. But it turned out that that policeman was a Jew. Now, that was an absolute rarity in Beuthen, a Jewish policeman. We got to know that man a little bit closer afterwards. He became a friend of the family. He was a Zionist. He wanted to go to Palestine. And he knew they needed in Palestine people eventually who knew how to handle guns. And in Germany, you couldn't buy a gun. And in Germany, it was hard to learn as a way to handle guns, other than to enlist in the army. But the German Army was restricted by-- restricted by the Contract of Versailles to 100,000 men. And you had to enlist for 12 years, or 10 years. Well, that was a little bit too long for Ralph. That-- his name was Ralph [? Rosensweich ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he enlisted in the police. That was the next good thing to do. And he was-- he learned how to handle guns and-- and so on. He was a good policeman. He came on Yom Kippur to the temple because they were assigning some policemen to [? Kattowitz. ?] So we went with a Gentile policeman, and he went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at one point, he was getting some correspondence, which he didn't want to come into-- the police over there, by the way, was living in barracks. And he had some mail, which he didn't want to come to the barracks. So that one day, that some of the mail came to my-- our house. It was before Easter. And my mother wanted to have it sent to him as quick as possible. So she went--
